weeks.weekly_summary: Foundation week establishing base aerobic fitness. Two easy runs on preferred days (Monday, Wednesday) to build aerobic capacity while respecting current fitness level. Focus on consistency and proper pacing.
weeks.coaching_notes: You are starting from a current easy pace of 6:10/km. This week, we establish consistency on your preferred running days (Monday and Wednesday). Strava shows recent low weekly volume (~15km), so Week 1 is conservative. All runs are at easy aerobic pace (Zone 2) to build aerobic base without fatigue. Cross-training on Tuesday (pilates) and Friday (gym) are locked in; Saturday football is blocked. Expect paces around 6:00–6:10/km this week—close to your current fitness.
weeks.next_week_preview: Week 2 will increase total volume by ~8–10% and introduce 1 quality session (tempo or intervals). Long run will grow to ~9km.

days.title: Rest Day
days.description: Complete rest to prepare for training week.

days.title: 🏋️ Gym Session
days.description: Light gym work: resistance training, core stability, mobility. Complement running with strength.
days.notes: Keep intensity light—this is a scheduled cross-training day.
days.coach_analysis: Good commitment to the plan—49 minutes of gym work with an average HR of 111 bpm suggests you kept the intensity controlled, which is exactly right for a cross-training day. This light-to-moderate effort will build supportive strength without hammering your central nervous system before your next running session. You're balancing the training week well.

days.title: ⚽ Football
days.description: Football match or training. High-intensity cross-training providing anaerobic stimulus.
days.notes: This is your scheduled weekly football commitment. Counts as cross-training stimulus.
days.coach_analysis: Solid execution of your football commitment—you maintained an aerobic-focused effort at 158 bpm average, which is right in that sustainable cross-training zone for building work capacity without compromising your running. The 5.75 km over 68 minutes shows you stayed engaged with good intensity variation typical of football, hitting the aerobic and leg-strength targets you planned for. This session delivered exactly what you needed.

days.title: Rest Day
days.description: Complete rest for recovery after football.

days.title: Easy 8km
days.description: Steady easy run at conversational pace ~6:05/km. Focus on relaxed cadence and aerobic base building. Heart rate should remain 65–75% max HR.
days.notes: Current fitness pace ~6:10/km; target this week is ~6:05/km.

days.title: 🧘 Pilates
days.description: Pilates session for core stability, flexibility, and movement quality. Low-intensity complement to running.
days.notes: Light cross-training; pairs well with running recovery.
days.coach_analysis: You substituted a restorative Pilates session with a hard evening run at 163 bpm average heart rate and 5:58/km pace—this is a significant deviation from the planned low-intensity cross-training. While the run itself looks solid, you've missed the opportunity for active recovery and mobility work that your body likely needs. Going forward, honor your cross-training days as planned, especially after harder running sessions.

coach_adjustment.change: Complete the Pilates session as originally planned before returning to running workouts
coach_adjustment.reason: Substituting structured recovery work with hard running increases injury risk and prevents the adaptations that cross-training provides

days.title: Easy 8km
days.description: Steady easy run at conversational pace ~6:05/km. Reinforce aerobic fitness and prepare for Week 2 progression. Maintain relaxed effort.
days.notes: Second easy run of the week. By Wednesday, you should feel recovered and strong.
days.coach_analysis: You swapped the planned easy run for a 44-minute pilates session—a smart cross-training choice that supports your running without the impact stress. Your average HR of 96 bpm stayed in the aerobic zone, maintaining fitness while giving your legs active recovery. This fits well into your training week, though make sure tomorrow's run feels fresh and you're not accumulating fatigue from back-to-back harder efforts.

weeks.weekly_summary: Volume increases by ~9% (from 16km → 17.5km). Introduce 1 quality session (tempo intervals) on Monday to begin threshold adaptation. Wednesday remains steady easy run. Cross-training continues (pilates, gym, football).
weeks.coaching_notes: Week 2 brings your first quality session: tempo intervals. On Monday, perform 5 × 2min @ ~5:35/km (close to current tempo estimate of 5:26/km, slightly conservative). This builds lactate threshold capacity. Wednesday stays easy at ~6:00/km to ensure recovery before weekend. Football on Saturday remains high-intensity stimulus. Progression is gradual—paces will improve over the block as fitness develops.
weeks.next_week_preview: Week 3 increases volume by ~6% and extends the long run to ~10km. A second quality session may be introduced if recovery is strong.

days.title: Rest Day
days.description: Complete rest to prepare for training week.
days.coach_analysis: Excellent work swapping your schedule flexibly—that's smart training management. You nailed the pace at 5:59/km (right on the 6:05/km target), but your average HR of 167.8 bpm ran considerably higher than the prescribed 65–75% max HR zone, suggesting you pushed the effort harder than intended despite hitting the right speed. This could indicate you were running into headwind, terrain challenges, or simply having a naturally elevated HR day—monitor this closely on your next easy run to ensure true conversational-pace recovery work.

coach_adjustment.change: Dial back intensity slightly or extend recovery focus; consider a shorter, genuinely conversational easy run
coach_adjustment.reason: HR running 20%+ above target zone suggests aerobic work was harder than planned—prioritize true easy-paced recovery before any harder efforts

days.title: 🏋️ Gym Session
days.description: Resistance training and core work. Light to moderate intensity.
days.notes: Scheduled cross-training day.

days.title: ⚽ Football
days.description: Football match or training providing high-intensity cross-training stimulus.
days.notes: Weekly football commitment.

days.title: Rest Day
days.description: Recovery day after football.
days.coach_analysis: You ran a moderate-paced 4.2km evening run at 147.7 bpm when a complete rest day was prescribed. While the pace and heart rate suggest controlled effort, this still represents active recovery rather than true rest after football. Consider honoring planned rest days more strictly to allow full CNS and muscular recovery—your body needs these days to adapt to training stress.

coach_adjustment.change: Add 5-10 min easy recovery pace if it's a hard session, or keep the next session unchanged if already marked easy
coach_adjustment.reason: Since you didn't fully rest today, ensure the next running session accounts for this incomplete recovery to avoid cumulative fatigue

days.title: Tempo Intervals: 5 × 2min @ 5:35/km + easy recovery
days.description: Warm-up: 2km easy @ 6:05/km. Main set: 5 × 2min hard @ 5:35/km with 1min easy recovery @ 6:15/km between reps. Cool-down: 2km easy @ 6:05/km. Total ~9.5km. Builds lactate threshold and tempo capacity.
days.notes: This is your first quality session. Maintain steady effort in the hard intervals—not all-out, but comfortably hard. Expect HR 80–85% max.

days.title: 🧘 Pilates
days.description: Pilates for core strength and mobility. Day after hard session—light recovery focus.
days.notes: Active recovery after Monday's quality session.

days.title: Easy 5km
days.description: Shorter, genuinely conversational easy run at relaxed pace. Focus on true recovery with HR staying within Zone 2 targets.
days.notes: Coach-adjusted: Reduced distance and duration to prioritize genuine easy-paced recovery. Dial back intensity—aim for comfortable, conversational effort. Keep HR in target zone.
days.adjustment_note: Dial back intensity slightly or extend recovery focus; consider a shorter, genuinely conversational easy run

weeks.weekly_summary: Peak week of Base Building phase. Volume increases by ~6% (17.5km → 18.5km). Introduce long run on Monday (~10km at easy pace), maintaining 1 quality session on Wednesday. Thursday active recovery preparation. Focus on building aerobic endurance while maintaining threshold fitness.
weeks.coaching_notes: Week 3 is the cumulative peak before Week 4 recovery. We shift strategy: Monday becomes a steady long run (~10km @ 6:00/km easy), building aerobic endurance. Wednesday features tempo intervals (4 × 3min @ 5:35/km) to maintain threshold fitness. Football on Saturday provides anaerobic stimulus. Volume is up ~6% from Week 2, but still conservative to prevent overtraining. Paces remain close to current fitness; by Week 4 recovery, you'll have a stronger aerobic base to build on in the Aerobic Development phase.
weeks.next_week_preview: Week 4 is RECOVERY: volume drops 20–25%, long run cut to ~8km, but maintain 1 quality session. Then transition to Aerobic Development (Week 5+) with progressive intensity and longer runs.

days.title: Rest Day
days.description: Complete rest to prepare for training week.

days.title: 🏋️ Gym Session
days.description: Resistance training, core work, mobility. Moderate intensity.
days.notes: Scheduled cross-training day.

days.title: ⚽ Football
days.description: Football match or training.
days.notes: Weekly football commitment.

days.title: Rest Day
days.description: Recovery after football.

days.title: Long Run 10km easy
days.description: Sustained easy run at ~6:00/km (conversational pace). Build aerobic endurance and capillary density. Relax and enjoy the run—this is your aerobic base builder for the half marathon.
days.notes: First long run of the block. Pacing should be relaxed; HR 65–75% max. Fuel and hydrate as needed.

days.title: 🧘 Pilates
days.description: Pilates for recovery and mobility. Day after long run.
days.notes: Active recovery; supports adaptation to long run.

days.title: Tempo Intervals: 4 × 3min @ 5:35/km + easy recovery
days.description: Warm-up: 2km easy @ 6:05/km. Main set: 4 × 3min @ 5:35/km with 1.5min easy recovery @ 6:15/km between reps. Cool-down: 2km easy @ 6:05/km. Total ~10km. Develops sustained threshold capacity for half-marathon racing.
days.notes: Longer intervals than Week 2—building aerobic power at threshold. HR 80–85% max in hard sections. Do NOT go all-out; maintain steady, sustainable effort.

weeks.weekly_summary: RECOVERY WEEK. Volume drops by ~20% (from 18.5km → 15.0km). Long run reduced to ~8km. Maintain 1 quality session (tempo) to preserve fitness, but overall focus is adaptation and rest. Prepare for Aerobic Development phase transition.
weeks.coaching_notes: Week 4 is mandatory recovery. Total volume drops ~20% to allow adaptation and supercompensation before Aerobic Development phase. Long run is 8km (vs 10km Week 3). Maintain 1 quality session (tempo intervals) on Wednesday to preserve threshold fitness, but reduce volume and intensity slightly. Football on Saturday is still part of schedule, providing cross-training stimulus. By end of Week 4, you'll be refreshed and ready for increased intensity and volume in Week 5+.
weeks.next_week_preview: Week 5 begins Aerobic Development phase (10 weeks to race). Increase volume by ~8–10%, introduce longer long runs (12km+), and build progressive tempo/interval sessions.

days.title: Rest Day
days.description: Complete rest to start recovery week.

days.title: 🏋️ Gym Session
days.description: Light resistance and mobility work. Reduced intensity for recovery week.
days.notes: Keep intensity light during recovery week.

days.title: ⚽ Football
days.description: Football match or training. Maintains cross-training stimulus despite reduced running volume.
days.notes: Weekly football commitment.

days.title: Rest Day
days.description: Recovery after football.

days.title: Long Run 8km easy
days.description: Easy steady run at ~6:00/km. Relaxed long run for recovery week—shorter than Week 3 to allow adaptation. Focus on conversational pace and feel.
days.notes: Reduced long run for recovery. HR 65–75% max. This should feel easy and enjoyable.

days.title: 🧘 Pilates
days.description: Pilates for recovery, flexibility, and core stability.
days.notes: Recovery-focused active recovery.

days.title: Tempo Intervals: 3 × 3min @ 5:35/km + easy recovery
days.description: Warm-up: 2km easy @ 6:05/km. Main set: 3 × 3min @ 5:35/km with 2min easy recovery @ 6:15/km between reps. Cool-down: 2km easy @ 6:05/km. Total ~9km. Maintain threshold fitness during recovery week with reduced volume.
days.notes: Fewer reps than Week 3 (3 vs 4) to keep volume down. Quality over quantity. HR 80–85% max. This preserves fitness while allowing recovery.

roadmap.weekly_summary: Transition to Aerobic Development. Volume increases ~8% from Week 4. Long run grows to 12km, building endurance. Tempo intervals expand to 5 × 2.5min to develop sustained threshold capacity. Begin bridging toward target paces (easy ~5:55/km, tempo ~5:30/km). Cross-training continues (pilates, gym, football).

Volume increases ~6% from Week 5. Long run progresses to 13km, building aerobic engine. Introduce fartlek session (6 × 2min hard @ 5:25/km / 2min easy recovery) to develop varied aerobic capacity. Maintain football and pilates. Begin small steps toward target paces.

key_sessions: Long Run 14km with 3 × 2km @ Marathon Pace (2:59/km) embedded
roadmap.weekly_summary: Transition to Peak Training phase. Volume rises ~5% from Week 6. Long run extends to 14km with marathon-pace segments (3 × 2km @ 2:59/km) to build race-specific fitness. Introduce VO2max intervals (6 × 1km @ 4:50/km) on separate day. Bridge paces closer to targets. Maintain cross-training.

key_sessions: Long Run 15km with 4 × 2km @ Marathon Pace (2:59/km) embedded
roadmap.weekly_summary: Peak Training continues. Volume increases ~4% from Week 7. Long run reaches 15km with extended marathon-pace segments (4 × 2km @ 2:59/km), building sustained threshold endurance. Tempo intervals strengthen lactate threshold (8 × 1.5min @ 5:15/km). Paces now close to target values. Football provides cross-training stimulus.

key_sessions: Long Run 15.5km with continuous 5km @ Marathon Pace (2:59/km)
roadmap.weekly_summary: Peak Training cumulative peak. Volume increases ~2% from Week 8. Long run reaches 15.5km with continuous 5km marathon-pace segment, developing sustained power at race intensity. VO2max intervals (5 × 1.2km @ 4:40/km) sharpen leg speed and aerobic capacity. Paces now at or near target values. Prepare for taper.

key_sessions: Long Run 14km with 6km continuous @ Marathon Pace (2:59/km)
roadmap.weekly_summary: Final Peak Training week before taper. Volume reduces ~6% from Week 9 (beginning taper protocol). Long run slightly shorter (14km) but maintains marathon-pace work (6km continuous). Race-pace repeats (10 × 1min @ 2:51/km) sharpen leg turnover and confidence at goal race intensity. All paces now at targets. Prepare for taper (Week 11+).

Begin 3-week taper. Volume drops ~22% from Week 10. Long run reduces to 11km at easy effort (5:45/km). Maintain intensity with 4 × 3min half-marathon pace repeats to keep legs sharp without accumulating fatigue. Light cross-training continues. Focus on sleep, nutrition, and mental preparation.

Mid-taper. Volume drops further ~17% from Week 11. Long run shortens to 9km, easy pacing (5:50/km). Maintain race-pace work (3 × 2min @ 2:51/km) to keep neuromuscular system sharp. Reduce cross-training intensity. Prioritize sleep and recovery.

Final taper week. Volume drops ~33% from Week 12. Focus on rest and freshness. Light easy run (6km) with strides (6 × 100m at fast leg turnover, not all-out) keeps legs responsive. Minimal cross-training. Mental focus: visualize race, confirm goal paces (2:51/km), maintain confidence. Friday-Saturday before Sunday race = minimal activity.

Race Week. Minimal running (5km total). Monday: light shakeout (3km easy @ 6:00/km). Wednesday: race-pace strides (4 × 100m). Friday: rest or 2km very easy. Saturday: complete rest, prepare logistics, confirm race plan. Sunday: Execute at target pace 2:51/km. Go race hard, trust your training.

coaching_overview: This 4-week Base Building block establishes your aerobic foundation and introduces threshold fitness for the half marathon. You start from recent running of ~15–16 km/week at 6:10/km easy pace, and current tempo estimate of 5:26/km. Week 1–3 build progressively (16 → 17.5 → 18.5 km) using polarized training (80% easy, 20% hard). Week 4 is recovery (15 km) to allow supercompensation. All paces begin close to current fitness (~6:05/km easy, ~5:35/km tempo in Week 2) and gradually progress toward targets (eventually ~5:45/km easy, ~5:15/km tempo by Week 6). Your weekly time budget is 4 hours; the plan respects this by using Monday and Wednesday for runs (2 sessions/week). Saturday football and fixed cross-training (pilates, gym) are blocked and integrated into the schedule. Quality sessions are introduced in Week 2 (tempo intervals), scaled in Week 3 (longer intervals), and reduced in Week 4 recovery. After this block completes, you'll enter Aerobic Development (Weeks 5–6) with longer runs and sustained threshold work, then Peak Training (Weeks 7–10) featuring marathon-pace segments and VO2max intervals, followed by a 3-week taper and race week. Your target race time is 2:00:00 (2:51/km pace); trust the plan and execute consistently.
weekly_summary: Foundation week establishing base aerobic fitness. Two easy runs on preferred days (Monday, Wednesday) to build aerobic capacity while respecting current fitness level. Focus on consistency and proper pacing.

Strava shows recent low weekly volume (~15km), so Week 1 is conservative. All runs are at easy aerobic pace (Zone 2) to build aerobic base without fatigue. Cross-training on Tuesday (pilates) and Friday (gym) are locked in; Saturday football is blocked. Expect paces around 6:00–6:10/km this week—close to your current fitness.
